title: Born in Bethlehem

prediction.text: But thou, Bethlehem Ephratah, though thou be little among the thousands of Judah, yet out of thee shall he come forth unto me that is to be ruler in Israel; whose goings forth have been from of old, from everlasting.

fulfillment.text: Now when Jesus was born in Bethlehem of Judaea in the days of Herod the king, behold, there came wise men from the east to Jerusalem.

notes: Micah wrote roughly 700 years before Jesus was born. Bethlehem was a tiny village, but the prophet specifies it by name — and by the qualifier 'Ephratah' to distinguish it from another Bethlehem in Zebulun.

title: Born of a virgin

prediction.text: Therefore the Lord himself shall give you a sign; Behold, a virgin shall conceive, and bear a son, and shall call his name Immanuel.

fulfillment.text: Now all this was done, that it might be fulfilled which was spoken of the Lord by the prophet, saying, Behold, a virgin shall be with child, and shall bring forth a son, and they shall call his name Emmanuel, which being interpreted is, God with us.

notes: Matthew explicitly cites Isaiah's sign as fulfilled in Jesus. The Hebrew almah and the Greek parthenos both carry the sense of a young woman of marriageable age, with parthenos in the LXX clearly meaning virgin.

title: The suffering servant

prediction.text: But he was wounded for our transgressions, he was bruised for our iniquities: the chastisement of our peace was upon him; and with his stripes we are healed.

fulfillment.text: Who his own self bare our sins in his own body on the tree, that we, being dead to sins, should live unto righteousness: by whose stripes ye were healed.

notes: Isaiah 53 is one of the most striking pre-Christian descriptions of substitutionary atonement. Peter quotes it almost verbatim and applies it directly to Jesus' crucifixion, written 700+ years after Isaiah.

title: Pierced hands and feet

prediction.text: For dogs have compassed me: the assembly of the wicked have inclosed me: they pierced my hands and my feet.

fulfillment.text: Where they crucified him, and two other with him, on either side one, and Jesus in the midst.

notes: Psalm 22 was written by David around 1000 BC, centuries before crucifixion was invented as a Roman execution method. Its details — pierced hands and feet, garments divided by lot, mockery from onlookers — read like an eyewitness account of the cross.

title: Not abandoned to the grave

prediction.text: For thou wilt not leave my soul in hell; neither wilt thou suffer thine Holy One to see corruption.

fulfillment.text: He seeing this before spake of the resurrection of Christ, that his soul was not left in hell, neither his flesh did see corruption.

notes: Peter's Pentecost sermon quotes Psalm 16 to argue that David was speaking prophetically about the Messiah's resurrection, since David himself died and was buried, but the Messiah did not see corruption.

title: The kingdom that crushes all others

prediction.text: And in the days of these kings shall the God of heaven set up a kingdom, which shall never be destroyed: and the kingdom shall not be left to other people, but it shall break in pieces and consume all these kingdoms, and it shall stand for ever.

fulfillment.text: And he shall reign over the house of Jacob for ever; and of his kingdom there shall be no end.

notes: Daniel's vision of four kingdoms (Babylonian, Persian, Greek, Roman) ends with an everlasting kingdom established 'in the days of these kings.' Jesus was born and ministered during the Roman empire — the fourth kingdom in Daniel's sequence.
